In genere, le sanzioni pecuniarie ove previste in alternativa alla riduzione in pristino (ad es., art. 33, co. 2 

e art. 34, co. 2, d.P.R. n. 380/2001) rappresentano perciò una specie di risarcimento per equivalente - 

anziché in forma specifica95 - dell’interesse leso dalla violazione delle prescrizioni edilizie96. 

La sanzione amministrativa pecuniaria conseguente all’inottemperanza all’ordinanza di demolizione (art. 

31, co. 4 bis, d.P.R. cit.) non è alternativa al ripristino dello stato dei luoghi, ma è finalizzata a punire il 

responsabile dell’inottemperanza, sì che condivide il carattere afflittivo della sanzione di acquisizione al 

patrimonio del comune97.  

La sanzione in oggetto consiste in uno strumento sia di coazione indiretta dei responsabili degli abusi dei 

quali sia stata constatata l’omessa demolizione sia di repressione della condotta omissiva di 

inottemperanza alla demolizione. La funzione di prevenzione generale e speciale ben si attaglia alla 

medesima, volta da un lato a salvaguardare l’assetto urbanistico del territorio nazionale e dall’altro a 

dissuadere dalla commissione d’illeciti edilizi98.  

Ha assunto perciò rilievo l’irretroattività delle norme sanzionatorie dell’illecito amministrativo, che in 

materia, come in quella penale, è un corollario del principio di legalità99 secondo cui “le leggi che prevedono 

sanzioni amministrative si applicano soltanto nei casi e per i tempi in esse considerati” (art. 1, co. 2, l. n. 689/1981; 

art. 2, d.lgs. n. 231/2001). Una fondamentale garanzia che si è estesa alle sanzioni amministrative a 

carattere punitivo (cfr. l. n. 689/1981), rispetto alle quali si impone la medesima esigenza di cui

Il principio in parola, seppur previsto per tutte le leggi (art. 11, co. 1, disp. prel. al cod. civ.), assume rango 

costituzionale soltanto rispetto alla materia penalistica101, con la conseguenza che il principio per l’illecito 

amministrativo può subire deroghe ad opera di leggi speciali, così come norme transitorie contenute in 

leggi di depenalizzazione prevedono l’applicazione retroattiva delle sanzioni amministrative, introdotte in 

sostituzione di quelle penali, a fatti commessi anteriormente alla data di entrata in vigore delle leggi 

stesse102.  

Sennonché il principio trova espressa menzione nella CEDU, ove è previsto che “nessuno può essere 

condannato per una azione o una omissione che, al momento in cui è stata commessa, non costituiva reato secondo il diritto 

interno o internazionale” (art. 7) e così anche nella Carta dei diritti fondamentali dell’U.E. (art. 49). Trattasi 

di norme sovranazionali o internazionali che assumono il ruolo di norme c.d. interposte, integranti il 

parametro di legittimità costituzionale che vincola il legislatore interno a esercitare la potestà normativa 

in conformità ai vincoli derivanti dall’ordinamento comunitario e dagli obblighi internazionali (art. 117, 

co. 1, Cost.). Ciò importa anche che, tra le plurime interpretazioni attribuibili alla disposizione interna, il 

giudice nazionale debba privilegiare quella maggiormente compatibile con i suddetti obblighi.  

La ratio del principio in questione è da ravvisarsi nella funzione di garanzia per il destinatario della norma 

sanzionatoria dell’illecito amministrativo. Si ritiene che il cittadino possa compiere libere scelte di azione 

soltanto se prima di agire possa sapere se la propria azione materiale od omissione integrerà una 

violazione - anche amministrativa - e in quale sanzione possa incorrere. Il divieto di applicazione 

retroattiva di sanzioni amministrative non previste al momento della commissione della violazione 

esprime dunque l’esigenza di calcolabilità delle conseguenze giuridico-sanzionatorie della propria 

condotta, quale condizione necessaria a fini della libera autodeterminazione individuale.

istantaneo ad effetti permanenti, si è affermato103 che la sanzione pecuniaria in esame non possa essere

The day after no tomorrow?: the right to seeds 

as a new and distinct human right?* 

 

by Francesco Seatzu 

Full Professor of International and European Union Law 

University of Cagliari 

 

Abstract [En]: The article delves into the emerging concept of the right to seeds as a potential new human right, 
particularly in the context of the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Peasants and Other People Working 
in Rural Areas (UNDROP). It highlights the efforts of environmental and human rights NGOs, as well as legal 
scholars, to advocate for the preservation and enhancement of peasants' rights to seeds in response to challenges 
such as seed enclosure and loss of agro-biodiversity. The discussion explores whether the right to seeds can be 
considered an instrumental right, primarily aimed at protecting intellectual property interests, or if it holds intrinsic 
value as a human right. Despite initial assumptions, the article argues that intellectual property and human rights 
logics are not inherently conflicting and may complement each other. Critically, the article examines Article 19 of 
the UNDROP, which addresses the right to seeds and agricultural knowledge. It analyzes the language and 
provisions of Article 19, highlighting both its potential strengths and shortcomings. While Article 19 represents a 
significant step in acknowledging peasants' rights to seeds, its vague and ambiguous language raises questions about 
its effectiveness in practice. In conclusion, the article underscores the importance of clarifying and strengthening 
the legal framework surrounding the right to seeds to ensure the protection of peasants' fundamental rights and 
freedoms. Despite the challenges and criticisms, the recognition of the right to seeds within international human 
rights discourse marks a significant milestone in the ongoing struggle for agricultural sovereignty and rural 
livelihoods. 
 
Titolo: Il giorno dopo il domani: il diritto alle sementi come nuovo e distinto diritto umano? 
Abstract [It]: L’articolo approfondisce il concetto emergente di diritto alle sementi come potenziale nuovo diritto 
umano, in particolare nel contesto della dichiarazione delle nazioni unite sui diritti dei contadini e delle altre persone 
che lavorano nelle aree rurali (UNDROP). Il documento evidenzia gli sforzi delle ong che si occupano di ambiente 
e di diritti umani, nonché degli studiosi di diritto, per sostenere la conservazione e il rafforzamento dei diritti dei 
contadini alle sementi, in risposta a sfide quali l'isolamento delle sementi e la perdita di agro-biodiversità. La 
discussione analizza se il diritto alle sementi possa essere considerato un diritto strumentale, volto principalmente 
a proteggere gli interessi della proprietà intellettuale, o se abbia un valore intrinseco come diritto umano. 
nonostante le ipotesi iniziali, l'articolo sostiene che le logiche della proprietà intellettuale e dei diritti umani non 
sono intrinsecamente in conflitto e possono completarsi a vicenda. L’articolo esamina criticamente l'articolo 19 
dell'undrop, che tratta il diritto alle sementi e alle conoscenze agricole. L’articolo analizza il linguaggio e le 
disposizioni dell'articolo 19, evidenziandone i potenziali punti di forza e le carenze. Sebbene l'articolo 19 
rappresenti un passo significativo nel riconoscimento dei diritti dei contadini alle sementi, il suo linguaggio vago e 
ambiguo solleva dubbi sulla sua efficacia nella pratica. In conclusione, l'articolo sottolinea l'importanza di chiarire 
e rafforzare il quadro giuridico relativo al diritto alle sementi per garantire la protezione dei diritti e delle libertà 
fondamentali dei contadini. Nonostante le sfide e le critiche, il riconoscimento del diritto alle sementi all'interno 
del discorso internazionale sui diritti umani segna una pietra miliare significativa nella lotta in corso per la sovranità 
agricola e i mezzi di sussistenza rurali.

Introduction 

Over the last two decades, and in particular since the beginning of the negotiations of the UN Declaration 

on the rights of peasants and other people working in rural areas (hereinafter ‘UNDROP’ or the ‘UN 

Declaration’) in the early 2000s,1 environmental and human rights non-governmental organisations 

('NGOs') like La Via Campesina2 and legal scholars from around the world (and especially from the so-

called ‘Global South’)3 have participated in legal advocacy surrounding seeds4. Such advocacy has 

aimed to preserve and enhance peasants' and farmers' rights to seeds and so to advance 'seed commons' 

and 'seed sovereignty', namely the peasants' and farmers' rights to save, breed and exchange seeds and to 

have access to diverse and open seed sources.5 These actors' action repertoires have come in response to

processes of seed enclosure and loss of agro-biodiversity and are almost always context-specific.6 Seed 

activism comes in several different forms, reflecting the profound diversity of farmers' seed networks, the 

common shared objective of challenging patents on genetically modified crops and also the multiplicity 

of the types of legal regime applicable to seeds.7 Lawsuits from farmers and/or from their representative 

organisations and transnational peasant activism aimed at establishing the right to seeds and agricultural 

knowledge as a fundamental right of peasants and other rural workers are good examples of legal 

mobilisations, namely of the process by which actors invoke legal norms, discourse, or symbols to 

influence policy or behaviour.8  

A number of key questions are gradually emerging from the international legal literature on seed activism, 

including that on the conditions for the patentability of plants and seeds9, which is of crucial importance 

if the peasants' right to seeds has already evolved into a new, distinct and balanced human right following 

its formal acknowledgment in the final text of the UN Declaration (Article 19).  

The aim of the present work is to investigate and answer this latter question (a question which is 

still under-researched, and therefore under-debated, at least in international legal writings).10 To achieve 

this purpose and aim, the closely connected issue of whether peasants' right to seeds is intrinsically an 

instrumental intellectual property (IP) right (and, thus, a right that cannot be qualified as a proper 'human 

right' because it is inherently ‘instrumental’ in its character)11 is examined and discussed first. 

 

1. Is the Right to Seeds an Instrumental Right? 

Is the right to seeds an instrumental right? The answer, in our view, is a definitive no, as explained below 

in detail. In fact, although the collective right to seeds may certainly be framed as an (instrumental) right

Though perhaps prima facie surprising, this latter possibility is supported by several important facts and 

arguments, including: (a) the fact that intellectual property logics and human rights logics are not truly 

incompatible as they are generally perceived and often described in legal writings14 (as is also suggested 

by the circumstance that human rights protection and intellectual property protection are two distinct 

fields that have largely evolved separately);15 (b) the fact that intellectual property rights (IPRs) are often 

justified as moral rights in accordance with the droit d'author concept;16 and (c) the fact that there are often 

practical difficulties in granting intellectual property protection to natural or legal persons17 who have 

managed to develop seeds and/or certain traits within plants.18 Each of these facts and arguments will be 

considered and discussed in turn.

First of all, why are human rights logics and IPR logics not opposites (as, for instance, is generally claimed 

by those legal scholars who contest that IP rights have human rights bases)19 but instead complementary 

to one another?  Several elements can be put forward to explain and answer this question. One of the 

most important of these elements is certainly that not only IP rights but also socio-economic human 

rights try to strike a proper 'balance' between access on one side and incentives on another. This is 

confirmed in particular, by the science and technology related provisions of the Universal Declaration of 

Human Rights (UDHR),20 as well as by Article 15 (1) (c) of the International Covenant on Economic, 

Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR).21  

A second element worth recalling is presented by Professor Philippe Cullet in her book 'Intellectual 

Property Protection and Sustainable Development', more precisely where the author contends that there 

is a strict relationship between IP rights and the realisation of the human rights of the poor in developing 

countries.22 A third, and strictly related, element to be mentioned has to do with the end of the isolation 

of IP rights from wider debates concerning their impact on the realisation of human rights or on 

environmental conservation following the adoption of the TRIPS Agreement.23 A fourth and final 

element has to do with the fact that there is a direct causal relationship between the granting of patents 

in the field of genetic engineering and the restriction of farmers’ fundamental rights and freedoms such 

as the right to seeds, the right to biodiversity and the right to access to adequate food.24 Of course, none 

of these elements and facts could, however, be used to exclude that IP rights may also work as obstacles 

to the effective implementation of human rights, i.e., in particular to the effective implementation of the 

right to public health and/or to contest that, while social human rights have enhanced access to 

necessities as their basic rationale, the IPR system has restricted access to new technologies and creative 

work as its basic rationale.25 To understand this point, one can recall in particular that patent holders

A second and equally decisive argument that can be put forward to counter the idea that the right to 

seeds is intrinsically an instrumental right concerns the fact that the right to seeds, like IP rights, may also 

be configured as a moral right (namely, as a non-instrumental right). Far from being controversial, the 

configuration of IP rights as both moral and legal rights is widely accepted in legal writings.27 Other than 

that (and most importantly) this is the configuration of these rights that is suggested and also supported 

by the text of the Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works (the Berne 

Convention), namely one of the most important international agreements governing copyright law.28 As 

defined by the Berne Convention, moral rights are the rights: “to claim authorship of the work and to 

object to any distortion, mutilation or other modification of, or other derogatory action in relation to, 

the said work".29  

Finally, a third argument that may be used has to do with the practical difficulties that surround the 

granting of intellectual property protection rights to the persons and companies who develop new types 

of seeds and/or certain traits within plants.30 This is because seeds and plants are not only commodities

systems. 

The section that follows will critically examine and discuss whether the right to seeds may be successfully 

framed as a new and distinct human right.

The focus here is on Article 19 of the UNDROP dealing with the right to seeds and to agricultural

interests of peasants and farmers concerning seeds with the interests of the holders of intellectual 

property (IP) rights over seeds, plants and related processes.

criticism here has to do with the formulation of Article 19, which largely repeats that of other articles of 

the UN Declaration and, in particular, of Article 19.5 that emphasises that States have a positive 

obligation to respect peasants' rights and choices in relation to their seeds and the crops and species that 

they wish to grow.  

Similar criticisms could also be raised in relation to Article 19.3 providing that: “States shall take measures 

to respect, protect and fulfil the right to seeds…”. The reason behind these criticisms is not difficult to 

understand: so formulated, Article 19.3 is a vague provision which does not set any particular benchmarks 

or place specific obligations and duties on the State and is unlikely to be effective in practice. 

Other criticisms could also be directed towards Article 19.4. The main reference is here to the fact 

that Article 19.4, by providing in "broad and generic language" that affordable seeds of sufficient quality 

and quantity shall be made available to peasants at an affordable price, offers very little to no guidance 

for its interpretation and application. Some guidance, however, can be provided from Article 19.6 holding 

that States: “shall take appropriate measures to support peasant seed system and promote the use of 

peasant seeds and agro-biodiversity”. And yet this is notwithstanding the great uncertainties 

that surround the meaning of the term “support” under Article 19.6. 

The remaining paragraphs of Article 19 (paras. 7 and 8) are dedicated almost entirely to clarifying that 

the peasants' seed system shall be supported, that agricultural research must integrate the needs of 

peasants and that IPR legislation must respect and take into account peasants' rights, needs and realities. 

Clearly, the most important of these provisions is Article 19.8 that refers explicitly to IP laws, by declaring 

that States shall ensure that such laws (i.e. intellectual property laws, certification schemes and seed 

marketing laws), “respect and take into account the rights, needs and realities of peasants…”. However, 

and regrettably, as currently formulated Article 19.8 does not include the reference to the wording “in 

particular the right to seeds…” that was earlier envisaged by the UNDROP drafters. As a result of this 

deletion (and especially of the broad and generic language used to describe the above-mentioned duty 

to respect and take into account the rights, needs and realities of peasants in the elaboration of the IPR 

laws), Article 19.8 does not deserve a better evaluation than the one awarded above to the previous 

provisions and paragraphs of Article 19. 

Notwithstanding a framing within the UN human rights institutional space, in a language of “rights” that 

the UN and its members may easily understand, peasants' claims to seeds rights under Article 19 stand 

more as expressions of righteousness rather than rights.57 It follows, then, that contrary to what has been

breeders and life science companies.60 

 

4. Concluding Remarks 

Some important points may be drawn from the analysis above: 

Firstly, the (individual and collective) right to seeds is not inherently an instrumental right as prima facie 

suggested by its insertion in the ITPGRFA, as an IP treaty;  

Secondly, there are a number of arguments and reasons indicating that the right to seeds may also be 

recognised and framed as a non-instrumental, but autonomous, (human) right. The most important of 

these arguments and reasons is perhaps that intellectual property logics and human rights logics are 

compatible and not in conflict, unlike what is often assumed in legal writings; 

Thirdly, notwithstanding this, and notwithstanding the explicit intention of the drafters of Article 19 of 

the UNDROP to characterise the right to seeds as a new and distinct human right, Article 19 does not 

look like a free-standing and self-executing human right provision. And this is mainly because of the 

structural vagueness and strong ambiguity of some of its most relevant provisions, notions and 

paragraphs. Indeed, what a missed opportunity for enhancing the protection of the fundamental rights 

and freedoms of peasant peoples!
